en.extract:
Des Murs's wiretail (Sylviorthorhynchus desmurii) is a small passerine bird of southern South America which belongs to the ovenbird family Furnariidae. Molecular phylogenetics places it within the Synallaxinae and indicates that the genus diverged from the Leptasthenura about 14–15 million years ago.


Taxonomy
Des Murs's wiretail was illustrated and given the binomial name Sylviorthorhynchus desmurii by the French naturalist Claude Gay in 1845. The specific epithet was chosen to honour the French ornithologist Marc Athanase Parfait Œillet des Murs. The species is monotypic: no subspecies are recognised.


Description
It is 24 centimetres (9.4 in) long, with the very long tail accounting for about two-thirds of this, but weighs as little as 10 grams (0.35 oz). The tail consists of just six feathers which are very narrow and filament-like: so few rectrices are elsewhere seen only in the emu-wrens of Australia. The two central feathers are greatly elongated while the two outer feathers are very short. The plain plumage is reddish-brown above, paler on the underparts. There is a pale stripe above the eye. The bird is small and rounded with a very fine bill. The song is fast and high-pitched.


Distribution and habitat
It is found in western Argentina from Santa Cruz Province north to San Juan and in southern and central Chile from northern Magallanes to Valparaíso Region. It inhabits dense thickets of Chusquea bamboo within cool temperate rainforests, occurring from sea-level up to 1,200 metres, but also can occur in early-successional shrublands of highly shade-tolerant Myrtaceae species, chiefly Amomyrtus. Des Murs's wiretail is also known to occur in dense weedy thickets of plants like gorse over the northern extremity of its range. Even in undisturbed rainforests, however, wiretails use only the low-level shrub and bamboo layer for foraging, never flying higher than 3 metres (10 ft) above the ground.


Behaviour and ecology
It is very shy and difficult to see, being completely unable to enter areas without dense vegetative overstoreys, and cannot typically use corridors smaller than 25 m (80 ft) or fly further than 50 m (160 ft) between patches of suitable habitat.
When encountered Des Murs's wiretail can easily be mistaken for a mouse. It has the unique ability among birds to curl up its long tail into a ball when alarmed.
The ball-shaped nest is made of plant fibres and built amongst vegetation close to the ground. Both partners are highly territorial with a territory size of around 1 ha (2.5 acres) per pair; however, pairs in territories smaller than 20 ha (49 acres) are seldom successful at nesting. This territory and the nest site are defended vigorously against conspecifics with a distinctive territorial song, and it is possible that the long tail is used by both sexes as an means of choosing a mate, though no actual tests have been used to determine how wiretails find a mate.
As is typical for south temperate insectivorous birds, during the breeding season of October to February Des Murs's wiretail lays a clutch of two to four eggs, but for the bird's mass these are about the largest eggs of any passerine bird. At 20.5 mm (0.8 in) long and 15.8 mm (0.6 in) wide, they weigh twice as much as expected for a 10 gram passerine and a clutch of four weighs more than the adult female – a feat equalled only by much more fecund kinglets. Little is known about incubation, fledging periods, or postfledging parental care due to the very secretive nature of the species.


References

Jaramillo, Alvaro; Burke, Peter & Beadle, David (2003) Field Guide to the Birds of Chile. Christopher Helm, London.


External links
Des Murs's wiretail in IBC (Internet Bird Collection.
Des Murs's wiretail sounds in Xenocanto.

'''Des Murs's wiretail''' ('''''Sylviorthorhynchus desmurii''''') is a small [[passerine]] [[bird]] of southern [[South America]] which belongs to the [[ovenbird (family)|ovenbird]] family Furnariidae. [[Molecular phylogenetics]] places it within the [[Synallaxinae]] and indicates that the genus diverged from the ''[[Leptasthenura]]'' about 14–15 million years ago.<ref>Gonzalez, J. & M. Wink (2008) Phylogenetic position of the monotypic Des Murs' Wiretail (''Sylviorthorhynchus desmursii'', Aves: Furnariidae) based on mitochondrial and nuclear DNA. ''Journal of Ornithology'' 149(3):393–398 doi 10.1007/s10336-007-0251-0</ref>

==Taxonomy==
Des Murs's wiretail was illustrated and given the [[binomial name]] ''Sylviorthorhynchus desmurii'' by the French naturalist [[Claude Gay]] in 1845.<ref>{{ cite book | last=Gay | first=Claude | author-link=Claude Gay | date=1845 | title=Historia fisica y politica de Chile. Atlas | volume=2 | location=Paris | publisher=E. Thunot y ca | at=Plate 3 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/27990704 }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | last=Gay | first=Claude | author-link=Claude Gay | date=1847 | title=Historia fisica y politica de Chile. Zoologica | volume=1 | location=Paris | publisher=En casa del autor | page=316 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/41662291 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal | last1=Gregory | first1=Steven | last2=Dickinson | first2=Edward | date=2012 | title=An assessment of three little-noticed papers on avian nomenclature by G. N. Kashin during 1978–1982 | journal=Zootaxa | volume=3340 | pages=44–58 [48–49] | doi=10.11646/zootaxa.3340.1.3 | url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/286020655 }}</ref> The specific epithet was chosen to honour the French ornithologist [[Marc Athanase Parfait Œillet des Murs]].<ref>{{cite book | last=Jobling | first=James A. | year=2010| title= The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names | publisher=Christopher Helm | location=London | isbn= 978-1-4081-2501-4 | page=134 | url=https://archive.org/stream/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling#page/n134/mode/1up }}</ref> The species is [[monotypic]]: no [[subspecies]] are recognised.<ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen | date=July 2023 | title=Ovenbirds, woodcreepers | work=IOC World Bird List Version 13.2 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/ovenbirds/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=28 October 2023 }}</ref>

==Description==
It is {{convert|24|cm|in|1}} long, with the very long tail accounting for about two-thirds of this, but weighs as little as {{convert|10|g|oz}}.<ref name="birds">Johnson, Alfredo William (author) and Goodall, J.D. (illustrator); ''The birds of Chile and adjacent regions of Argentina, Bolivia, and Peru'', Volume II; pp. 171–173.</ref> The tail consists of just six [[feather]]s which are very narrow and filament-like: so few rectrices are elsewhere seen only in the [[emu-wren]]s of Australia.<ref>Sibley, Charles Gald and Alquist, John E.; ''Phylogeny and Classification of the Birds: A Study in Molecular Evolution''; p. 576. {{ISBN|0300040857}}</ref> The two central feathers are greatly elongated while the two outer feathers are very short. The plain [[plumage]] is reddish-brown above, paler on the underparts. There is a pale stripe above the eye. The bird is small and rounded with a very fine [[beak|bill]]. The [[bird song|song]] is fast and high-pitched.

==Distribution and habitat==
It is found in western [[Argentina]] from [[Santa Cruz Province (Argentina)|Santa Cruz Province]] north to [[San Juan Province (Argentina)|San Juan]] and in southern and central [[Chile]] from northern [[Magallanes y Antártica Chilena Region|Magallanes]] to [[Valparaíso Region]]. It inhabits dense thickets of ''[[Chusquea]]'' [[bamboo]] within cool [[temperate rainforest]]s, occurring from sea-level up to 1,200 metres, but also can occur in early-successional shrublands of highly shade-tolerant [[Myrtaceae]] species, chiefly ''[[Amomyrtus]]''.<ref name="mating">Diáz, Iván A., Armesto, Juan J. and Willson, Mary F.,[http://www.captura.uchile.cl/jspui/bitstream/2250/2672/1/Diaz%20IA.pdf "Mating success of the endemic Des Murs' Wiretail (''Sylviorthorhynchus desmursii'', Furnariidae) in fragmented Chilean rainforests"]{{Dead link|date=January 2024 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }}</ref> Des Murs's wiretail is also known to occur in dense [[weed]]y thickets of plants like [[gorse]] over the northern extremity of its range.<ref name="mating"/> Even in undisturbed rainforests, however, wiretails use only the low-level shrub and bamboo layer for foraging, never flying higher than {{convert|3|m|ft|0}} above the ground.

==Behaviour and ecology==
It is very shy and difficult to see, being completely unable to enter areas without dense vegetative overstoreys,<ref>Sieving, Katharyn E, Willson, Mary F. and de Santo, Toni L.; [http://www.sendadarwin.cl/espanol/wp-content/uploads/2010/04/corridors-for-birds-in-chiloe.pdf "Defining Corridor Functions for Endemic Birds in Fragmented South-Temperate Rainforest"]</ref> and cannot typically use corridors smaller than {{convert|25|m|ft|-1|abbr=on}} or fly further than {{convert|50|m|ft|-1|abbr=on}} between patches of suitable habitat.<ref name="observaciones">Diáz, Iván A., Willson, Mary F., Gehee, Steven M.C. & Armesto, Juan J.; [http://www.aveschile.cl/boletin/PDF/12/44-49-BCO12-(2006)-IDiaz-biologia-conservacion-colilarga.pdf "Observaciones Sobre La Biologia y Conservación del Colilarga (Sylviorthorhynchus desmursii, Furnariidae) en la Isla de Chiloé, Chile"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304064548/http://www.aveschile.cl/boletin/PDF/12/44-49-BCO12-(2006)-IDiaz-biologia-conservacion-colilarga.pdf |date=2016-03-04 }}</ref>

When encountered Des Murs's wiretail can easily be mistaken for a [[mouse]]. It has the unique ability among birds to curl up its long tail into a ball when alarmed.<ref name="birds"/>

The ball-shaped [[bird nest|nest]] is made of plant fibres and built amongst vegetation close to the ground. Both partners are highly territorial with a territory size of around {{convert|1|ha|acre|1|abbr=on}} per pair; however, pairs in territories smaller than {{convert|20|ha|acre|0|abbr=on}} are seldom successful at nesting.<ref name="mating"/> This territory and the nest site are defended vigorously against conspecifics with a distinctive territorial song,<ref name="observaciones"/> and it is possible that the long tail is used by both sexes as an means of choosing a mate,<ref>See Moreno, Juan, Merino, Santiago, Lobato, Elisa, Rodríguez-Gironés, Miguel A. and Vásquez Rodrigo A.; "Sexual dimorphism and parental roles in the thorn-tailed rayadito (Furnariidae)" in ''[[The Condor (journal)|The Condor]]'' 109(2):312–320 (2007)</ref> though no actual tests have been used to determine how wiretails find a mate.

As is typical for south temperate insectivorous birds, during the breeding season of October to February Des Murs's wiretail lays a clutch of two to four eggs,<ref name="observaciones"/> but for the bird's mass these are about the largest eggs of any passerine bird. At {{convert|20.5|mm|in|1|abbr=on}} long and {{convert|15.8|mm|in|1|abbr=on}} wide,<ref name="birds"/> they weigh twice as much as expected for a 10 gram passerine<ref>Based on the formula of egg mass = 0.258''m''<sup>0.73</sup>, where ''m'' is body mass. From Rahn, H., Sotherland, P. and Paganelli, C. V., 1985. "Interrelationships between egg mass and adult body mass and metabolism among passerine birds" in ''Journal für Ornithologie'' 126:263–271.</ref> and a clutch of four weighs more than the adult female – a feat equalled only by much more fecund [[kinglet]]s. Little is known about incubation, fledging periods, or postfledging parental care due to the very secretive nature of the species.

==References==
{{Reflist}}
*Jaramillo, Alvaro; Burke, Peter & Beadle, David (2003) ''Field Guide to the Birds of Chile''. Christopher Helm, London.
